turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Alito has the opinion of the Court this morning in case 18-6210, Mitchell v. Wisconsin.

turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: The question in this case is whether a person who is arrested for drunk driving and is unconscious may be given a blood test without obtaining a warrant. This is the third case decided by the Court in recent years regarding efforts made by the states to combat drunk driving. From the mid-1970s to the mid-1980 driving accidents took an average of 25,000 lives a year. Today the rate is 60% lower. One likely cause of the decline is tougher enforcement of laws prohibiting driving with a blood alcohol level that exceeds a certain threshold. These laws now exist in all 50 states and the District of Columbia and to help enforce them every state also requires drivers to submit to testing of their blood alcohol level if they are suspected of drunk driving. Such a test normally takes one of two forms, a breath test or a lab analysis of the blood sample taken from the driver. But only blood draws are available for unconscious drivers, who are in no condition to blow into a breathalyzer. That was true of petitioner Gerald Mitchell. In May of 2013 police responded to reports that Mitchell had been drinking and driving. An officer soon found Mitchell wandering near a beach, slurring his words and barely able to stand. A preliminary breath test showed a blood alcohol level of .24%; that is triple the legal limit in Wisconsin, where Mitchell was arrested. Indeed, Mitchell was so drunk that he soon fell unconscious. That left the police no chance to conduct a more reliable breath test using better equipment back at the station. So they transported Mitchell to the hospital and ordered a blood test, and that test administered about 90 minutes after his arrest showed a blood alcohol level of .222. Mitchell moved to suppress the results of that test. He argued that it ran afoul of the Fourth Amendment since it was conducted without a warrant. Under our case law, blood alcohol tests qualify as Fourth Amendment searches and in general a lawful search does require a warrant, but there are exceptions. For instance, the exigent circumstances exception allows police to conduct a warrantless search when evidence is about to be destroyed and there is no time to seek a warrant. Because evidence of a driver's blood alcohol level naturally dissipates as the alcohol is metabolized, this exigency exception is sometimes triggered in drunk driving cases. We now hold that in cases involving unconscious motorists, this exception will as a general rule permit a warrantless blood draw. The blood test is the only way to enforce drunk driving laws against suspects who pass out, surely the most dangerous class of drivers. And there will often be no time to seek a warrant because of the urgent health, safety, and law enforcement needs that arise in cases involving such drivers. For one thing, the unconscious driver's condition is itself a medical emergency. It almost always requires the driver to be rushed to the hospital, not just for a blood test, but for urgent medical care. Police can expect that such a driver might require monitoring on the way to the hospital, that his blood may be drawn anyway for diagnostic purposes immediately on arrival, and that urgent medical treatment could delay or distort a blood test performed later upon receipt of a warrant, which would make the test results less reliable and less useful. On top of that, drivers who are so drunk as to lose consciousness are likely to crash; it was a minor miracle that this did not happen in Mitchell's case, especially if they pass out before they manage to park. And then the accident might give officers a slew of other urgent tasks, like calling for medical care for the injured, or providing first aid themselves, or dealing with fatalities, or preserving evidence at the scene, or blocking and redirecting traffic to prevent further accidents. These duties would often pose a dilemma. Police would have to choose between prioritizing critical health and safety needs or conducting effective tests to enforce laws that save lives. That is just the kind of scenario for which the exigency rule was born. For that reason the exigency exception will normally allow a warrantless blood test when a driver's mental state prevents a standard breath test. Perhaps in rare cases a defendant may be able to show that his blood would not have been drawn if police had not been seeking information about his alcohol level and that the police could not have reasonably judged that the warrant application would interfere with other pressing needs or duties. Because Mitchell had no chance to attempt to make that showing, a remand for that purpose is necessary. We therefore vacate the judgment of the Supreme Court of Wisconsin and remand for further proceedings. Justice Thomas has filed an opinion concurring in judgment. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justices Ginsburg and Kagan join. Justice Gorsuch has also filed a dissenting opinion.
